what_it_does: Defines an elevation spec for a generative design renderer, documenting observed gaps between declared DNA language and current rendered behavior, then mapping fixes into renderer wiring and missing DNA/team-contract asks.
observations: Opens with a mandate that generated screens should surpass a static-reference floor while resolving through an existing DNA language.
Centers the document on one shared renderer, `DNA.renderArchetype`, as the leverage point for changes across multiple surfaces.
Leads with a device-accuracy section that distinguishes correct measured aspect ratio from missing DNA-sourced device tokens.
Organizes gaps by severity labels and repeatedly separates each fix into existing-engine wiring and missing-DNA authoring.
Names specific resolvers, tokens, JSON files, CSS rules, and runtime line references as evidence for each claim.
Ends with team asks grouped under composition, projection, and recollection, followed by a ceiling statement for live DNA-driven rendering.
standouts: The core interpretation is that many visual and behavioral misses come from a frozen renderer that does not call available DNA resolvers.
The document treats host handoff of tokens, grammar, and dial positions as the gating step for resolution-first rendering.
It converts design-language ideas into operational contracts: bonds, surfaces, voice forms, ordinal ramps, texture hooks, motion registers, and device tokens.
conventions: Uses mandate, verdict, measured evidence, target/mine comparison, capability fix, team-ask, and ceiling sections.
Labels implementation work as `(a)` wire-to-existing-engine and `(b)` author-the-missing-DNA.
Uses prose audits with inline file and line citations rather than code snippets.
Frames renderer changes as file-local capability wiring and contract changes as team-owned asks.
Includes correction notes where earlier readings are described as stale or unsupported.
concerns: The file combines audit findings, implementation plan, ownership routing, and architectural doctrine in one long document.
Several assertions rely on cited runtime measurements and line references, but the document itself does not include reproducible commands or captured output.
Some terms such as floor, ceiling, DNA, voice forms, and movement layer are domain-specific and depend on surrounding project vocabulary.
notes: Brief this as a command spec that turns a generative-renderer design audit into resolver-wiring tasks and DNA contract asks.
questions: What exact host option schema should be used when passing tokens, grammar, and dial positions into `renderArchetype`?
Where should voice lint warnings appear during build or render?
Which build step is expected to emit `--dna-device-w` and `--dna-device-h`?
What is the accepted schema location for new bond, surface, voice_form, texture, connector, and state-register declarations?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the actual content: it specifies how to elevate the generative floor by routing rendering through declared DNA resolvers and identifying missing contracts.
apparent_themes: state, voice, motion, texture, and surface as generative systems
intention_signals: It appears intended to align implementation teams around moving design decisions out of one-off markup and into shared renderer wiring plus authored DNA declarations.
novelty: It treats aesthetic qualities such as warmth, ordinal emphasis, zoning, voice, texture, and motion as resolvable runtime substrates rather than static presentation choices.
contribution: Contributes a neutral ledger of renderer elevation requirements, observed unresolved substrates, host handoff needs, and DNA contract additions that a fused system would preserve as traceable design-system obligations.
summary_for_embedding: A generative floor elevation spec auditing `DNA.renderArchetype` against a DNA design language and mapping unresolved spacing, color, ordinal, zoning, voice, texture, motion, and device-size behavior to renderer wiring and contract asks.
intention_for_embedding: This file was written to guide teams toward a single resolution-first renderer where generated surfaces inherit proportions, colors, surfaces, voice checks, textures, motion, and device sizing from shared DNA data.
